The only point we should discuss is whether we want to enable this endpoint by default, or have the user explicitly enable it (e.g., `ENABLE_DIAGNOSTICS`, similar to what we're doing with `ENABLE_CONFIG_UPDATES`). We do strongly recommend our users to always only bind the edge port to 127.0.0.1, but that may not always be the case, unfortunately.

I guess the main issue is that we'd want to enable the diagnostics for an already running instance - because it may be hard to re-create a faulty state, and we don't want to make it a requirement to re-start the container (to enable the environment config). One option would be to allow the user to `docker exec localstack_main touch /tmp/.enable-diagnostics`, which could then override the `ENABLE_DIAGNOSTICS` environment variable.

Don't want to over-engineer the solution here, but security could definitely become a concern here, and we should make a conscious decision.

Overall the direction makes sense, a little confusing though so I just wanted to clarify.

Previously we had three types of task
(1) Actor creation with dynamic options
(2) Actor creation
(3) Task
And two types of worker:
(A) dedicated worker
(B) worker

(1) used its own map (A), while (2) and (3) drew from the list (B).

- Prior to this PR, we could actually reuse a (3) worker for (2).  But in the current PR, we disallow this. Is this intended / is my understanding correct?
- Prior to this PR, we had a map for (A), which allowed (1) to look up the specific worker it needed. (The worker process needed to have been started with the correct command line options `dynamic_options`). But in the current PR, we got rid of (A) and don't seem to check for the specific worker anywhere (I would expect this logic to appear near where we scan through the list of workers and "skip if the runtime env doesn't match".)  So it seems like (1) could pop an arbitrary worker that didn't have the correct dynamic options. Is this an issue?

I did a first pass and left some comments. Apart from that, I think it's better to have a `_with_retries` wrapper for all the api methods, for the following reasons:
1. Keep things consistent: in the current state, some api methods do retries while some don't, it's confusing for the developer
2. Keep the option to use the api methods without the retries for all api operations
3. Smaller code changes: easier to reason about them, less error prone

Additionally, we should be very careful to not alter the existing error handling logic while adding the 429 retries.

> in a sync thread too!

This was slightly intentional, although obviously not documented very well. SHA256 hashing is not the absolute fastest thing, and so doing it in parallel is a good idea. Doing it inside of `store_bytes` meant that it was running on a `tokio` blocking thread (which there are [more of](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/8935264eea79ffd8cbd40b7067e4f2216a1aae28/src/python/pants/option/global_options.py#L1826-L1833)), rather than on what we call a "core" thread (the threads that `tokio` runs async code on).

So... you might want to benchmark this with the default value of https://www.pantsbuild.org/v2.15/docs/reference-global#rule_threads_core, and with 2x the default?

Could you update the development installation instructions to explain how to set up pre-commit?

I've also found that people can sometimes find pre-commit frustrating when hooks cannot automatically fix the issues they discover. For example, `black` works great as a pre-commit step for most people because it automatically fixes any stylistic mistakes, but `flake8` can be annoying being it reports the error without fixing it and will block you from committing until you do. If the commit is really just a WIP and you don't want to fix everything you have to know to go look at the pre-commit docs to know how to turn off its checks for that commit. 

Pre-commit checks like `flake8` are fine to run in CI, but maybe not by default on ever commit a developer might make as they're writing code. Given this, it looks like pre-commit allows you to configure when hooks will run [using `stages`](https://pre-commit.com/#confining-hooks-to-run-at-certain-stages). Perhaps for those hooks which do not automatically correct mistakes they could be marked as `manual` and only run in CI.

IMO the best (most readable) way would be to always ensure the `template`-variable is set, set to None if necessary.

So all the way at the top we can say:
```
if name:
    template = ...
elif template_id:
    template = ..
else:
    template = None
```

The other changes then become more obvious:
```
if versions and template is not None:
   ...
elif template is not None:
    ret_versions = template.versions
```

I've gone ahead and kicked off the rest of the buildkite builds to make sure the existing tests are indeed passing.

The only outstanding test I'd like to see is one that proves if we pass `profile_name` in a config, an S3 resource is actually constructed the way we'd like it to be. [`test_s3_file_manager.py::test_s3_file_manager_resource`](https://github.com/Deveshi/dagster/blob/197d1f44f36d8ceb94feaef01220f179926d1ed3/python_modules/libraries/dagster-aws/dagster_aws_tests/s3_tests/test_s3_file_manager.py#L159) is already really close to what we want. But you'll notice that if you were to modify the config being used in that test to include a `profile_name`, that you'd suddenly have a failing test instead of a passing one:

```diff
diff --git a/python_modules/libraries/dagster-aws/dagster_aws_tests/s3_tests/test_s3_file_manager.py b/python_modules/libraries/dagster-aws/dagster_aws_tests/s3_tests/test_s3_file_manager.py
index 54d2683ce..14bfbeeb5 100644
--- a/python_modules/libraries/dagster-aws/dagster_aws_tests/s3_tests/test_s3_file_manager.py
+++ b/python_modules/libraries/dagster-aws/dagster_aws_tests/s3_tests/test_s3_file_manager.py
@@ -165,6 +165,7 @@ def test_s3_file_manager_resource(MockS3FileManager, mock_boto3_resource):
         "endpoint_url": "http://alternate-s3-host.io",
         "s3_bucket": "some-bucket",
         "s3_prefix": "some-prefix",
+        "profile_name": "foo",
     }
 
     mock_s3_session = mock_boto3_resource.return_value.meta.client
```

```
botocore.exceptions.ProfileNotFound: The config profile (foo) could not be found
```

I'd recommend creating a new test in the same file called `test_s3_file_manager.py::test_s3_file_manager_resource_with_profile`. That test should include your new `profile_name` configuration.

I think there are two equally good options for proving that our code is working as expected when given a profile name:

1. During the test setup, create an AWS credentials file with your profile. [Similar to what's being recommended here](https://github.com/spulec/moto/issues/2634#issuecomment-734112339). Assert that a resource is successfully created using the profile in that credentials file.
2. Assert that we get a `botocore.exceptions.ProfileNotFound` error if we try to pass a profile that doesn't exist. This is probably the simpler option and I think it sufficiently proves that our code is correctly trying to use the `profile_name` config. [Pytest has a `pytest.raises()` method that you can use to make this assertion](https://docs.pytest.org/en/6.2.x/assert.html#assertions-about-expected-exceptions).

one thing I am thinking about is if the new API makes sense... seems like a weird merging of the exiting `encode_trace`/`encode_traces` API and the new buffering approach

`encoder.put()` will encode a single trace, and then `encoder.encode()` will join all previously encoded traces?

The contract is that a buffered encored can hold some data via `put`, and gives you the encoded result with `encode`. When and how the data is actually encoded is an implementation detail. The reason why part of the encoding needs to happen during the `put` is because of the buffer size constraint (and also because cython could potentially hog the CPU for a long time if too many traces are encoded at the same time, with no ticker mechanism in place to regularly release the GIL).

At a higher level and more future looking, I think that we might benefit from changing the block future abstraction from `ObjectRef[Block]` with a `BlockMetadata` sidecar object to `Block[Union[Data, ObjectRef[Data]], Metadata]`, where the block wraps a data (future) object and block metadata that's known at `Block` instantiation time, and exposes pretty much the same APIs. I think that something like that would simplify a lot of the remaining implementation, where we're currently carting around both block (future) objects and their corresponding metadata, and we're trying to surface metadata `Block` APIs for an `ObjectRef[Block]` via a separate `BlockMetadata`, which is clunky.

Hey Devaraj.

Apologies for the thrash, but I spent some more time thinking about it, and I think we might be better off not merging this change for a number of reasons.

1. The `key` in `since_key` is specifically referring to the s3 string key.  It wouldn't really describe a timestamp.
2. We could still introduce a timestamp-based field, but I think you'd be better off using a a generic string cursor (introduced in `0.11.10` rather than using the `last_completed_time` (which we've since deprecated).  The primary reason for this is we don't want to introduce a set of bugs based on clock-skew for timestamps calculated using different machines.  We'd be comparing timestamps calculated within the sensor daemon process with timestamps calculated on the remote S3 server.  It'd be easy for there to be some inconsistencies where we wouldn't trigger runs correctly in all situations.
3. Instead of `get_s3_keys`, maybe we'd want to have something that returns a list of the full s3 boto objects (including `Key` and `LastModified`.  We could then use that last modified string value as the cursor.  Something like this:

```
@sensor(pipeline_name="my_pipeline")
def s3_sensor_by_timestamp(context):
    new_s3_content = get_s3_contents(MY_BUCKET, MY_PREFIX, modified_after=context.cursor)
    if not new_s3_content:
        return SkipReason('no new s3 content')
    # we found new s3 content, get the most recent one, and update the cursor
    most_recent = sorted(new_s3_content, reverse=True)[0]
    if most_recent['LastModified']:
        context.update_cursor(most_recent['LastModified'])
```

  - Adding special casing in our code for JupyterHub is a large

    know what it is an how to test changes on it. Our build, test, and

    down to the TensorBoard binary. In particular, we do not attempt to
    parse, inspect, or modify the arguments, and this is by design.

    arbitrary plugin code in the host IPython environment, would
    introduce an interdependency between the host `%tensorboard` and the
    target binary, etc.

better on these two constraints?

Thanks @matt-boris for looking into this. This seems fine to me, but is also a bit weird.

If I go to Firefox-CI, I get two options:

![Screen Shot 2022-01-25 at 9 17 34 AM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/286017/151004539-15859834-2bc1-4a0f-879d-7b868af6ff26.png)

If I chose "Sign in with Auth0", then I go through Mozilla's SSO workflow in a new tab. The tab auto-closes, so I can't see if it calls `/login/oauth/authorize`. But maybe this is the one getting rate limited. The `POST` is done in the user's browser, so it would be associated with the user's IP (used by [express-rate-limit](https://github.com/nfriedly/express-rate-limit)).

If you chose "Sign in with credentials", you enter a Client ID and Access Token. These are turned into an authentication headers, and you do not `POST` to `/login/oauth/authorize`. The token is checked when used.

If you sign in on community-tc, the option is "Sign In with Github". In a similar way, a tab is opened, and I suspect it calls `POST` to `/login/oauth/authorize` before closing. Maybe this could be confirmed in the server logs.

So, the potential negative is that valid users will log in with SSO or GitHub, and get rate limited on the final step of the OAuth workflow. If the users are using a Proxy, this is more likely to happen. I think our corporate VPN or other VPNs will look like a proxy, so employees are more likely to see this issue. There are settings to [trust one or more levels of proxy](https://github.com/nfriedly/express-rate-limit#troubleshooting-proxy-issues) to make this less likely. Still, 200 in one minute is a lot!

This mitigation will rate limit requests pretending to be the final steps of an OAuth workflow. Or valid final steps of an OAuth workflow. It does not rate limit:

* Guessing the access tokens for static client IDs
* Guessing OAuth credentials

In other applications, I would add a report of 429 rate limits seen in a day, to see if this worked at all (or negatively impacted actual customs). Maybe we add that as a TODO item for the monitoring project.

Hmm, it's a bit too bad to have to replicate all of the setup and infrastructure for the various flavors of summary API in the separate test file.  Here's an idea - we could keep the test part of the single source file, but make it not run by default, and define a separate py_test() rule that _does_ run that test.

I think we could do this as follows:

- Add logic to the test case that skips the large count test unless the `TEST_SIZE` environment variable is "large" (https://docs.bazel.build/versions/main/test-encyclopedia.html#initial-conditions)
- In the separate `py_test()` rule, we make that one `size = "large"` and we set the `args` parameter (https://docs.bazel.build/versions/main/be/python.html#py_test) to the fully qualified name of the test method we want to run. This will get passed to the test-specific `main()` function and should result in only that test method being run: https://docs.python.org/3/library/unittest.html#unittest.main

Here are some test results from my machine. The master+preroll example (second one) shows that the current version on master does indeed create new TCP connections when filtering out HLS segments while not consuming the response content afterwards.

All tests have set `--hls-segment-stream-data` set to true (via `--twitch-low-latency`), which is what's causing the issue (like in the linked threads above). Filtering segments and not consuming the response content is just another issue which this PR solves.

According to python-requests / urllib3, connections which are fully consumed get released back to the connection pool:

- https://github.com/psf/requests/blob/v2.26.0/requests/models.py#L758-L759
- https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/blob/1.26.7/src/urllib3/response.py#L555-L579
- https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/blob/1.26.7/src/urllib3/response.py#L520-L530

hey there 

interesting idea, i think "reader client" was probably added for the SentinelRedisBackend, but sure. 

however, we have a lot of arguments that set up the client, not just URL and host.   if you want readerclient to be a separate client, basically **every argument** has to be duplicated for reader and there would need to be a separate call to _create_client() to consume all those arguments.

this suggests a huge amount of duplication taking this approach, so I suggest a separate dictionary entirely of connection arguments that will be consumed by a second call to _create_client.


**example one - writer is a single host, reader refers to a pre-establihsed connection pool** - this is very plausible that reader connections might use some entirely different means of load balancing etc. 

```py
from dogpile.cache import make_region

region = make_region().configure(
    "dogpile.cache.redis",
    arguments={
        "host": "localhost",
        "port": 6379,
        "db": 0,
        "redis_expiration_time": 60 * 60 * 2,  # 2 hours
        "thread_local_lock": False,
        "reader_client": {"connection_pool": some_redis_pool},
    },
)
```

**example two - SSL is used** - this is nearly guaranteed if someone is using SSL, that you might need different certs for reader/writer hosts

```py

from dogpile.cache import make_region

region = make_region().configure(
    "dogpile.cache.redis",
    arguments={
        "host": "localhost",
        "port": 6379,
        "connection_kwargs": {
            "ssl": True,
            "ssl_certfile": "writer_file.crt"
        }
        "reader_client": {
            "host": "some_other_host",
            "connection_kwargs": {
                "ssl": True,
                "ssl_certfile": "reader_file.crt"
            }

        },
    }
)
```

we also require unit tests for all PRs, so here you would work these out in test_redis.py -> RedisConnectionTest.      that said this is a good idea for a change so thanks!

yeah, synced offline. I think the plan overall looks great. main concern is that splitting into more shards but without a story that pulls them together makes me a bit worry that it might be challenging to resolve them properly later.

so ideally, I would like to see that:

- the resolved pip-compile'd file somewhere, with all packages versions pinned
- a set of test somewhere that checks that the resolved version list satisfies every shard, ideally outside the docker build process.
- a brief doc of steps somewhere about how to add/update a dependency (how to recompile/regenerate the resolved, pinned version file)

Overall looks good, thanks @oke-aditya. I have a few comments inline. 

Apart from them, there is the larger issue of annotating inputs with `nn.Module` rather than a more precise type. In general, we should make the input types as lenient and the output types as strict as possible. Meaning if an `nn.Module` has all teh interfaces we need within our implementation, we shouldn't force a specific type as input. On the other hand, if we return two possible subtypes of `nn.Module`, it would be better to return `Union[Foo, Bar]` instead of `nn.Module`. The former case carries more information for static type checkers to work with.

Note that we need to relax that general rule a bit, since we operate on `torch.jit > mypy`. For example, in some cases `Sequence` would be a much better annotation than `List` or `Tuple`, but since `torch.jit` does not (yet?) support `Sequence` we can't use it. You can run our test suite to see if there are any issues with this.
